bullets.text: GRNs are networks of TFs + cis-regulatory elements that control gene expression cascades
Explain how one genotype → many phenotypes; critical for understanding body plan evolution
Transcription factors (TFs), cis-regulatory elements (CREs/enhancers), signaling molecules, target genes
Master regulators: Pax6 (eye), tinman (heart), Nkx2.5
TF binds enhancer → recruits co-activators → RNA Pol II transcribes target gene
Cascades = upstream TF activates downstream TFs → amplification
Modular enhancers allow tissue-specific expression
CRE vs. promoter: CRE can be far away, tissue-specific; promoter is proximal, required for all expression
Activator vs. repressor TFs
Pleiotropic vs. modular gene: pleiotropy = one gene many effects; modularity = different enhancers drive different expression domains
Mutation in CRE = tissue-specific change without lethal effect (evolvable)
Mutation in TF coding region = pleiotropic disaster
GRN rewiring = morphological evolution without new genes
"If you mutate an enhancer that drives limb-specific expression of gene X, what happens to gene X expression in other tissues?" → Answer: unchanged in other tissues — enhancer is tissue-specific
"Why are CRE mutations more likely to be preserved by selection than TF coding mutations?" → Answer: modular, non-pleiotropic effects
CREs do NOT code for protein — they are regulatory sequences, not coding sequences
Enhancers can work in any orientation and at great distance — not just near the promoter
Pax6 is a master regulator but IS NOT the only gene needed for eye development — it initiates the cascade
Gene duplication provides raw material for evolution — one copy maintains original function, other can diverge
Mechanism behind novelty without losing existing function
Paralogs (same species, different function from duplication), orthologs (different species, same function from speciation)
Neofunctionalization (new function), subfunctionalization (split ancestral functions), pseudogenization
WGD (whole genome duplication) or tandem duplication → duplicate released from constraint → mutations accumulate → divergence
Recruitment = coopting a gene for a new role
Gene promiscuity = gene expressed in new tissue via new CRE before duplication
Paralog vs. ortholog: duplication vs. speciation event separates them
Neofunctionalization vs. subfunctionalization: new role vs. split ancestral role
Gene recruitment vs. de novo gene: coopting existing vs. creating new
Pseudogenization if duplicate loses both sub-functions
Dosage imbalance after WGD can be lethal
Relaxed constraint on duplicates → accelerated evolution

"Crystallins in the eye lens were recruited from metabolic enzymes — is this neofunctionalization or gene recruitment?" → Answer: gene recruitment/co-option — enzyme function retained, lens function added
"If two proteins share 60% sequence identity in vertebrates and invertebrates, are they orthologs or paralogs?" → Answer: likely orthologs — separated by speciation
Paralogs are NOT the same as orthologs — paralogs arise from duplication within a lineage, orthologs from speciation
Subfunctionalization is NOT the same as neofunctionalization — subfunctionalization splits existing functions, neofunctionalization creates a new one
High sequence similarity does NOT prove orthology — could be paralogs that have not diverged
Hox genes = master regulators of body axis positional identity
Heterochrony = evolutionary change in developmental timing
Deep homology = same gene network in wildly different structures across phyla
Hox gene clusters (HoxA-D in vertebrates), homeodomain TFs
Paedomorphosis (adult looks like juvenile), peramorphosis (adult has more exaggerated features)
Bicoid, Nanos in Drosophila; Hh, Wnt, BMP signaling
Hox genes expressed in nested domains along AP axis; anterior Hox = anterior structures; posterior Hox = posterior
Heterochrony changes rate/onset of developmental programs
Neoteny = retention of juvenile features into adulthood; progenesis = sexual maturity at juvenile stage
Paedomorphosis vs. peramorphosis: juvenile features retained vs. adult features elaborated
Neoteny vs. progenesis: different mechanisms for paedomorphosis
Deep homology vs. analogy: same gene network vs. same structure from different genes
Hox mutations = homeotic transformations (leg where antenna should be)
Heterochrony drove human evolution (neoteny — large brain, flat face)
Deep homology means toolkit genes are ancient and conserved
"Axolotls remain aquatic and retain gills throughout life due to neoteny — is this paedomorphosis or peramorphosis?" → Answer: paedomorphosis, specifically neoteny
"If Drosophila and vertebrates both use Pax6 for eye development but have structurally different eyes, what does this indicate?" → Answer: deep homology — same gene network, convergent structures
Hox genes specify IDENTITY not pattern — they say "this region is a leg" not "build a leg from scratch"
Deep homology ≠ structural homology — different structures can share the same genetic toolkit
Neoteny is ONE type of paedomorphosis, not the only type — do not conflate them
Evolution is NOT unlimited — developmental, phylogenetic, and physical constraints channel evolution
Convergence and parallelism reveal that similar problems → similar solutions
Imperfect adaptations prove history matters
Developmental constraints (can't make structures without developmental toolkit)
Convergent evolution (independent origins, different genes), parallel evolution (independent origins, same genes)
Imperfect adaptations (e.g., recurrent laryngeal nerve, panda's thumb)
Constraints = certain mutations are not producible or are lethal
Convergence occurs when selection independently favors same phenotype via different genetic paths
Parallelism = same molecular solution independently; imperfect adaptations = historical baggage
Convergent vs. parallel evolution: different genes vs. same genes/mechanism
Constraint vs. selection: constraint limits options; selection chooses among options
Vestigial vs. imperfect adaptation: non-functional remnant vs. functional but suboptimal
Constraints explain "missing" morphologies in nature
Imperfect adaptations are evidence against design and FOR evolution
"Ichthyosaurs and dolphins independently evolved streamlined bodies — is this convergent or parallel?" → Answer: convergent evolution, different lineages, different genes
"The panda's thumb is not a true thumb — what does this illustrate?" → Answer: evolutionary constraint + imperfect adaptation — had to work with existing carpals
Convergent evolution does NOT mean identical genes — same phenotype can arise from different genetic paths
Constraints do NOT prevent evolution, they channel it — there is still plenty of evolutionary variation
Imperfect adaptations are evidence FOR evolution, not against selection — they show historical contingency
Sexual reproduction is costly (2-fold cost of males) yet ubiquitous — must have major benefits
Two main hypotheses: Muller's Ratchet (asexual accumulates deleterious mutations irreversibly) and Red Queen (sex generates diversity to stay ahead of coevolving parasites)
Muller's Ratchet: irreversible accumulation of deleterious mutations in asexuals
Red Queen hypothesis: arms race with parasites favors rare genotypes
Cost of sex (2-fold cost of males), recombination
Muller's Ratchet: asexual lineage can only lose alleles, not regain them; sex + recombination shuffles alleles, purges deleterious combos
Red Queen: parasites adapt to common host genotypes → rare genotypes have advantage → sex maintains genotypic diversity
Muller's Ratchet vs. Red Queen: mutation load vs. parasite coevolution
Sexual vs. asexual: diversity vs. reproductive efficiency
Cost of sex vs. benefit of recombination
Asexual populations accumulate mutations faster (Muller's Ratchet)
Red Queen supported by snail experiments (Potamopyrgus — more sexual in parasite-rich environments)
Most eukaryotes have some sexual stage
"New Zealand snails reproduce sexually more often in lakes with high trematode parasite loads — which hypothesis does this support?" → Answer: Red Queen hypothesis
"Why can't an asexual population easily evolve to have fewer deleterious mutations?" → Answer: Muller's Ratchet — without recombination, can only accumulate, not purge bad alleles
Muller's Ratchet is about DELETERIOUS mutations, not beneficial ones — do not confuse with positive selection
Red Queen does NOT predict parasites will win the arms race — it predicts a coevolutionary dynamic
Anisogamy (large egg vs. small sperm) → different optimal strategies for each sex → sexual selection
OSR (operational sex ratio) determines which sex competes more intensely
Anisogamy (size difference in gametes), OSR (ratio of sexually active males:females)
Intrasexual selection (same-sex competition for mates), intersexual selection (mate choice by opposite sex)
Bateman's principle (males benefit from multiple matings more than females)
Eggs are costly → females become choosy; sperm are cheap → males compete
OSR male-biased → strong male-male competition; OSR female-biased → female competition
Bateman's gradient = slope of reproductive success vs. number of mates (steeper = stronger selection)
Intrasexual vs. intersexual selection: competition vs. choice
OSR vs. sex ratio at birth: operational vs. overall
Bateman's principle vs. parental investment theory: gamete size argument vs. total investment argument
When males provide more care (pipefish, seahorses), females are showier
OSR can shift within a species (e.g., sex-role reversals)
Bateman's principle overgeneralized — females also benefit from multiple mates in some species

"In pipefish, males carry eggs in a brood pouch and females compete for males — how does this fit OSR theory?" → Answer: OSR is female-biased because males are the limiting resource → female-female competition, females more ornamented
"Why does Bateman's gradient explain stronger sexual selection on males in most species?" → Answer: male RS increases with more mates; female RS plateaus → steeper gradient for males
Anisogamy → different strategies, NOT that females are always choosy — OSR can override this
Bateman's principle is about VARIANCE in reproductive success, not just number of matings
Intersexual selection (mate choice) can be driven by runaway selection (arbitrary preference + trait coevolve) OR honest signals (costly display = reliable indicator of quality)
Fisher's runaway: positive feedback between female preference gene and male trait gene
Honest signals: costly signals correlated with genuine quality
Zahavian handicap: cost IS the signal — you can't fake expensive traits
Runaway: rare females prefer long tails → long-tail males have more offspring → preference gene and long-tail gene become genetically correlated → runaway until viability cost stops it
Handicap: only genuinely healthy males can survive while producing costly ornament → ornament = reliable quality indicator
Runaway vs. honest signal: arbitrary vs. condition-dependent
Handicap vs. signal detection: cost of production vs. cost of assessment
Fisher runaway vs. good genes: arbitrary preference vs. preference for actual genetic quality
Peacock's tail = classic honest signal (or runaway)
El Nino disrupts condition-dependent displays
Runaway can lead to maladaptive extremes (Irish elk antlers)

"A male bird with a bright red beak has more RS but is also more susceptible to parasites — does this support runaway or honest signaling?" → Answer: honest signaling — parasites create a cost, so only parasite-resistant males can maintain red beaks
"What would happen to peacock tail evolution if females suddenly chose randomly?" → Answer: runaway stops; directional selection for tail size ends; viability selection shortens tails
Fisher's runaway does NOT require that the trait be good for survival — it's ARBITRARY
Handicap principle does NOT mean all costly traits are honest — costs must be condition-dependent
Mating systems reflect ecological constraints + sexual conflict + parental investment
Sperm competition and cryptic female choice operate AFTER mating
Monogamy, polygyny (1 male:many females), polyandry (1 female:many males), promiscuity
Sperm competition (multiple males' sperm compete), cryptic female choice (female controls fertilization post-mating)
Testes size (proxy for sperm competition intensity)
Sperm competition → males evolve larger testes, more sperm per ejaculate, sperm traits that attack rivals
Cryptic female choice → females can bias fertilization toward preferred male's sperm via uterine environment
Mating system tracks OSR + resource distribution (resource-defense polygyny, female-defense polygyny)
Sperm competition vs. cryptic female choice: male vs. female control post-mating
Polygyny vs. polyandry: male vs. female has multiple mates
Resource-defense vs. female-defense polygyny: territory-based vs. female-group-based
Humans have intermediate testes size → mild sperm competition
Gorillas (harems) have tiny testes; chimpanzees (promiscuous) have enormous testes
"Chimpanzees have relatively larger testes per body mass than gorillas — what does this indicate?" → Answer: chimps have higher sperm competition → more promiscuous mating
"What is cryptic female choice and why is it hard to study?" → Answer: female controls fertilization after copulation; hard to study because it's internal and post-mating
Large testes indicate SPERM COMPETITION, not sexual selection per se — they are proxies for promiscuity
Cryptic female choice occurs AFTER copulation — it is not the same as pre-copulatory mate choice
Monogamy is not always due to mate guarding — it can be due to biparental care requirements
Male and female fitness interests often conflict (interlocus contest evolution, ICE)
Adaptations in one sex trigger counter-adaptations in the other → arms race WITHIN a species
Interlocus sexual conflict (conflict between alleles at different loci in each sex)
Intralocus sexual conflict (same allele has different optimal expression in each sex)
Drosophila seminal fluid proteins (Sfps/Acps)
Male evolves to manipulate female (e.g., seminal fluid that increases oviposition but harms female lifespan)
Female evolves resistance; intralocus conflict → sexually antagonistic alleles maintained at intermediate frequency
Interlocus vs. intralocus conflict: different loci conflict vs. same locus different optimum
Sexual conflict vs. mutualism: opposing vs. aligned interests
Arms race within species vs. between species (coevolution)
Drosophila SFPs increase female egg-laying but decrease female lifespan
Female genome evolves resistance to male manipulation
Drives rapid evolution of reproductive proteins

"Drosophila females that mate with males producing high levels of seminal proteins die younger but produce more offspring — whose fitness is being maximized?" → Answer: neither straightforwardly — male fitness increased (more eggs), female fitness may decrease (shorter life) → classic sexual conflict

"What is intralocus sexual conflict and why is it hard to resolve?" → Answer: same allele is good in one sex, bad in other → selection cannot fix one without harming the other; can be partially resolved by sex-limited expression
Sexual conflict does NOT mean males and females physically fight — it's about allele-level selection pressures
Seminal fluid proteins do NOT always harm females — harm is context and dosage specific
Life history = schedule of growth, reproduction, survival; trade-offs arise because resources are finite
Classic model: how fast to reproduce vs. how long to live
Stearns' opossum experiment, r vs. K selection (simplified), fast vs. slow life history continuum
Trade-offs: reproduction vs. survival, current vs. future reproduction, number vs. size of offspring
Opossums on island (low predation) evolved slower reproduction, longer lifespan in just 5,000 years
Predation drives fast life history; when predation low → invest in soma (repair/maintenance)
r-selected vs. K-selected: many small offspring vs. few large offspring; high vs. low extrinsic mortality
Intrinsic vs. extrinsic mortality: aging vs. predation
Semelparity vs. iteroparity: one big reproductive bout vs. repeated
Humans are K-selected: low extrinsic mortality → long lifespan, few offspring, high parental investment
Pacific salmon are semelparous (die after spawning)
Extrinsic mortality is the key driver of life history evolution

"Island opossums reproduced slower and lived longer than mainland opossums in Stearns' study — what does this tell us about predation and life history?" → Answer: low predation → survive longer anyway → selection favors investing in soma/repair → slower reproduction

"Why do annual plants reproduce all at once and die, while perennial plants reproduce repeatedly?" → Answer: annuals face high extrinsic mortality + unpredictable environment → semelparity maximizes current reproduction
r vs. K selection is oversimplified — do not use as the only framework; extrinsic mortality is the key variable
Slow life history is NOT always better — depends on extrinsic mortality rate
Parental investment theory (Trivers 1972) links investment per offspring to sex roles
Trivers-Willard hypothesis predicts sex-biased parental investment based on offspring condition
Trivers' parental investment theory, Trivers-Willard hypothesis
Sex-role reversal species (pipefish, seahorse, jacana), maternal vs. paternal care
The sex that invests more per offspring becomes the choosy sex; the sex that invests less becomes the competing sex
Trivers-Willard: mothers in good condition bias toward sons (who can use quality advantage to get many mates), mothers in poor condition bias toward daughters
Trivers-Willard vs. local resource competition: quality-dependent sex bias vs. resource-competition-based bias
Parental investment theory vs. Bateman: investment predicts choosiness; Bateman predicts from gamete size
Direct vs. indirect benefits to mate choice
Red deer mothers in good condition have more sons
Pipefish males carry eggs → OSR female-biased → females compete

"A red deer hind in excellent body condition is more likely to produce a male calf according to Trivers-Willard — why?" → Answer: sons of high-quality mothers can achieve high RS because male RS has high variance; daughters achieve moderate RS regardless of quality
"In seahorses, males get pregnant — what does parental investment theory predict about sex roles?" → Answer: males invest more → males become choosy; females compete for access to males
Trivers-Willard is about CONDITION-DEPENDENT sex ratio bias, not just producing equal sons and daughters
Parental investment ≠ time spent with offspring — includes ANY cost to parent (energy, risk, future reproduction)
Parent-offspring conflict: offspring want more resources than optimal for parent to give (Trivers 1974)
Genomic imprinting: parent-of-origin gene expression, explained by kinship theory
Trivers' parent-offspring conflict, Hamilton's relatedness (r)
Genomic imprinting, Igf2 (paternal, promotes growth), Igf2r (maternal, suppresses growth)
Prader-Willi and Angelman syndromes
Offspring is equally related to itself (r=1) but only r=0.5 to siblings; parent is equally related (r=0.5) to all offspring → conflict
Paternally-imprinted genes promote offspring growth (father's genes not in future sibs); maternally-imprinted genes suppress growth (mother's genes ARE in future sibs)
Genomic imprinting vs. epigenetics: imprinting IS epigenetic but has specific kinship explanation
Prader-Willi (maternal imprint failure → paternal copies overexpress) vs. Angelman (paternal imprint failure → maternal copies overexpress)
Parent-offspring conflict vs. kin selection: both invoke r but different mechanism
Prader-Willi syndrome: hyperphagia (overgrowth drive from paternal genes unleashed)
Angelman syndrome: developmental delay
Imprinting breakdown in cancer

"Igf2 is paternally expressed and promotes fetal growth. Igf2r is maternally expressed and degrades IGF2 signal. How does kinship theory explain this?" → Answer: paternal genes maximize current offspring growth because father may not share genes with future sibs; maternal genes moderate growth to preserve resources for future offspring

"Why do offspring demand more resources than parents are selected to give?" → Answer: offspring is equally related to itself vs. 0.5 to siblings; parent is 0.5 related to all → optimal transfer point differs
Genomic imprinting does NOT mean the silenced copy is deleted — it is present but methylated/silenced epigenetically
Igf2 is paternally expressed (NOT maternally expressed) — this is a common confusion
Why do organisms age and die? Two theories: mutation accumulation (deleterious late-acting mutations escape selection) and antagonistic pleiotropy (genes good early are bad late)
Menopause as adaptive life history
Medawar (mutation accumulation theory), Williams (antagonistic pleiotropy), disposable soma theory (Kirkwood)
Menopause, grandmother hypothesis
Mutation accumulation: genes expressed only late in life face little purifying selection → accumulate
Antagonistic pleiotropy: gene beneficial at age 20 that causes cancer at 70 is favored by selection because most die before 70
Disposable soma: invest in reproduction now, let soma (body) deteriorate
Mutation accumulation vs. antagonistic pleiotropy: passive accumulation vs. active selection for bad-late genes
Senescence vs. aging: senescence = decline in fitness with age; not all organisms senesce
Menopause adaptive vs. by-product: grandmother hypothesis vs. reproductive constraint
Calorie restriction extends lifespan → supports disposable soma
BRCA1/2 mutations increase cancer risk — potential antagonistic pleiotropy?
Grandmother hypothesis: post-reproductive females increase inclusive fitness by helping grandchildren

"A gene that increases testosterone (better reproduction at 25) also increases prostate cancer risk at 65 — what evolutionary theory does this illustrate?" → Answer: antagonistic pleiotropy — selection acts strongly on age 25, weakly on age 65

"Why do whales (and humans) have menopause while other mammals don't?" → Answer: grandmother hypothesis — ecological conditions where old female's help to grandoffspring outweighs her own reproduction
Mutation accumulation does NOT predict genes specifically bad at old age — any late-acting deleterious mutation accumulates
Antagonistic pleiotropy requires the gene to be BENEFICIAL early, not just neutral — the selection must be positive
Coevolution = reciprocal evolutionary change between interacting species
Can produce arms races, geographic mosaics, and coevolutionary hotspots/coldspots
Ehrlich & Raven (plant-butterfly coevolution), geographic mosaic theory of coevolution (Thompson)
Coevolutionary hotspots (coevolution occurs) vs. coldspots (no coevolution)
Garter snake-newt arms race (tetrodotoxin)
Interacting species exert reciprocal selection pressures → each adapts to the other → arms race
Geographic mosaic: interactions vary spatially → coevolution only where selection pressures overlap
Garter snakes evolve TTX resistance → newts evolve more TTX → local adaptation
Coevolution vs. correlated evolution: reciprocal selection vs. shared history
Hotspot vs. coldspot: active coevolution vs. none
Arms race vs. mutualism coevolution: escalation vs. mutualistic improvement
Evolutionary escalation (both species get better at offense/defense)
Stalemate when arms race is too costly
Geographic mosaic explains why one population has high resistance and neighbor doesn't

"Garter snake populations near newts with high TTX have higher TTX resistance than snake populations far from newts — what coevolutionary concept does this illustrate?" → Answer: geographic mosaic of coevolution — coevolutionary hotspot near newts, coldspot far from them
"Why doesn't the newt-snake arms race escalate forever?" → Answer: physiological limits + cost of TTX production + cost of nerve resistance — both sides eventually hit constraints
Coevolution does NOT require that both species benefit — arms races are coevolution too
Geographic mosaic does NOT mean coevolution occurs everywhere — just WHERE selection pressures overlap
Mimicry = resemblance to another species that benefits the mimic
Batesian (harmless mimic resembles harmful model) and Müllerian (two harmful species resemble each other)
Batesian mimicry (harmless mimic + harmful model + trained predator)
Müllerian mimicry (two unpalatable species converge), mimicry ring
Automimicry (individual mimics palatable to unpalatable form of own species)
Batesian: predator learns to avoid model → avoids mimic by association (mimic free-rides on model's warning)
Müllerian: two unpalatable species converge → predator only needs to learn ONE pattern → shared benefit
Selection for convergence stronger when both species abundant
Batesian vs. Müllerian: parasitic/deceptive vs. mutualistic; model harmed vs. mutual benefit
Automimicry vs. Batesian: within species vs. between species
Mimicry vs. camouflage: resemblance to predator's mental model vs. resemblance to background
Batesian mimics become rarer → model swamped → predators learn mimic is "safe" → mimicry breaks down if mimic too common
Müllerian mimicry stable and self-reinforcing
Monarch-viceroy (used to be textbook Batesian — now thought to be Müllerian)

"If a Batesian mimic population becomes very common relative to its model, what happens?" → Answer: predators encounter mimic more, learn it is harmless → mimicry breaks down → selection against mimic

"Why is Müllerian mimicry considered mutualistic while Batesian is considered parasitic?" → Answer: Müllerian — both species benefit from predator learning one pattern; Batesian — model is harmed when predators associate warning with harmless mimic
Monarch and Viceroy used to be textbook Batesian but viceroy is ALSO unpalatable — likely Müllerian
Batesian mimicry does NOT require that species are related — they just need to look alike
Mutualism = both partners benefit; endosymbiosis = symbiont lives inside host
Virulence evolution = how pathogen virulence evolves relative to transmission mode
Endosymbiosis theory (Margulis), mitochondria from α-proteobacteria, chloroplasts from cyanobacteria
Mycorrhizal fungi, rhizobia (nitrogen fixation), virulence-transmission trade-off
Endosymbiosis: free-living prokaryote engulfed → became organelle (genome reduction, host-dependent)
Virulence evolution: host mobility required for transmission → selection favors lower virulence; vector-borne disease → vector does the work → high virulence can evolve
Obligate vs. facultative mutualism: can't live without vs. beneficial but not required
Endosymbiosis vs. horizontal gene transfer: whole genome vs. individual genes
Virulence vs. pathogenicity: damage to host vs. ability to infect
Myxomatosis in rabbits — initially high virulence → rabbits die fast → virus can't spread → evolution toward intermediate virulence
HIV becomes less virulent when transmitted mostly sexually (host mobility needed)

"Myxoma virus in Australian rabbits evolved to be less lethal over time — what evolutionary principle explains this?" → Answer: virulence-transmission trade-off — killing host too fast prevents transmission; selection favored intermediate virulence
"What evidence supports the endosymbiotic origin of mitochondria?" → Answer: own circular genome, similar to α-proteobacteria, double membrane, divide by binary fission, 70S ribosomes
Mitochondria arose from α-proteobacteria, NOT just any gram-negative bacteria — specificity matters
Mutualism does NOT require that partners evolved together — can be recent and opportunistic
Lower virulence is NOT always favored — depends on transmission route
Tinbergen's 4 questions: mechanism (proximate-how), development (proximate-how), function (ultimate-why), evolution (ultimate-why)
Behaviors are phenotypes subject to selection; even organisms without brains show "behavior"
Proximate causes (mechanism, development), ultimate causes (function, phylogeny)
Behavioral phenotypes, slime mold maze solving, Venus flytrap, sea anemone
Proximate = what physiological/developmental processes produce the behavior
Ultimate = what is the adaptive value/evolutionary history
Same behavior can be explained at both levels (complementary, not competing)
Proximate vs. ultimate explanation: mechanism/development vs. function/evolution
Function vs. adaptive value: immediate benefit vs. fitness consequence
Individual selection vs. group selection: levels of explanation
Asking only proximate OR only ultimate is incomplete — both are needed
No-brain behavior challenges anthropocentric definition of behavior
"A male stickleback attacks a red-colored intruder because rising testosterone triggers aggressive circuits — is this proximate or ultimate?" → Answer: proximate — hormonal mechanism
"Why do male sticklebacks attack red objects in the first place?" → Answer: ultimate — males with red bellies are rivals; aggression has fitness consequences
Proximate and ultimate explanations are COMPLEMENTARY, not competing — one does not replace the other
Behavior does NOT require a brain — plants and slime molds show behavior-like responses
Function does NOT mean purpose — it means fitness consequence of the behavior
ESS (evolutionarily stable strategy) = strategy that cannot be invaded when rare
Game theory models behavioral evolution; individual vs. group selection debate
Individual selection (Williams), group selection (Wynne-Edwards, mostly rejected), ESS (Maynard Smith)
Hawk-Dove game, Prisoner's Dilemma, tit-for-tat
ESS: a strategy is an ESS if, when adopted by most, it cannot be beaten by rare alternatives
Hawk-Dove: Hawk (fight always) vs. Dove (retreat) → ESS is a mixed strategy
Tit-for-tat is ESS in iterated Prisoner's Dilemma
Individual vs. group selection: most traits evolve by individual selection; group selection requires very specific conditions
ESS vs. Nash equilibrium: ESS is evolutionary stability; NE is strategic stability
Frequency-dependent vs. frequency-independent selection: ESS vs. directional
Group selection once proposed to explain altruism — now largely replaced by kin selection and reciprocal altruism
Hawk-Dove predicts polymorphism at ESS frequency
Tit-for-tat explains cooperation in iterated games

"In Hawk-Dove where Hawks are rare, Hawks always win fights — what happens as Hawks become more common?" → Answer: Hawks increasingly fight each other, paying fight costs → ESS equilibrium where frequency of Hawks stabilizes

"Why is group selection considered a 'special case' rather than a general mechanism?" → Answer: requires high group-level variation AND low individual selection coefficient — easily undermined by cheaters within groups
Group selection is NOT universally rejected — it's accepted under specific conditions (e.g., structured populations)
ESS is not necessarily the optimal strategy for the individual — it is evolutionarily stable, not individually optimal
Hamilton's rule: altruism evolves when rB > C (r=relatedness, B=benefit to recipient, C=cost to actor)
Inclusive fitness = direct fitness + indirect fitness from relatives
Hamilton's rule (rB > C), inclusive fitness, coefficient of relatedness (r)
Full sib (r=0.5), half sib (r=0.25), parent-offspring (r=0.5)
Alarm calls in ground squirrels, worker bees
Altruistic gene spreads if benefit to relatives (weighted by r) exceeds cost to actor
Alarm calls in ground squirrels — caller at risk but saves relatives
Worker bees give up reproduction (C high) but increase queen's reproduction (B very high, r=0.75 in haplodiploidy)
Kin selection vs. group selection: gene-centric through kin vs. group-level selection
Direct vs. indirect fitness: own offspring vs. via relatives
Hamilton's rule vs. reciprocal altruism: relatedness vs. reciprocity mechanism
Explains warning calls, cooperative breeding, eusociality
Fails when r is low or when actors cannot discriminate kin
Bacteria can perform kin selection via quorum sensing

"A ground squirrel emits an alarm call (C=0.3), warning 4 full sibs (r=0.5, B=0.1 each). Does Hamilton's rule predict the call should evolve?" → Answer: rB = 0.5×0.1×4 = 0.2; C = 0.3; rB < C → does NOT evolve with these values
"Why are worker bees related to queen's offspring by r=0.75?" → Answer: haplodiploidy — males are haploid → workers share 100% of father's genes + 50% of mother's → r=0.75 to sisters
Hamilton's rule predicts when altruism CAN evolve — not that it WILL always evolve
Inclusive fitness is NOT the same as group fitness — it is individual fitness plus effects on relatives
Kin selection does NOT require kin recognition — familiarity or location cues work
Eusociality = overlapping generations + cooperative brood care + reproductive division of labor
Evolved multiple times (bees, wasps, ants, termites, naked mole rats)
Eusociality definition (3 criteria), haplodiploidy (males haploid)
r=0.75 among sisters in Hymenoptera, sterile worker caste, queen
Naked mole rat (diploid eusocial mammal)
Haplodiploidy → sisters more related to each other (r=0.75) than to their own offspring (r=0.5) → Hamilton's rule favors helping raise sisters over personal reproduction
BUT termites are diploid and eusocial → haplodiploidy is NOT sufficient or necessary
Fortress defense, assured fitness returns, ecological constraints also drive eusociality
Eusociality vs. cooperative breeding: full 3 criteria vs. just cooperative brood care
Haplodiploidy vs. diploid eusociality: termites, mole rats
Altruistic workers vs. mutualistic workers: indirect fitness vs. direct benefit
Eusociality is evolutionarily rare but enormously ecologically successful (ants = 20% of terrestrial animal biomass)
Queen-worker conflict ongoing; worker policing; reproductive cheating in workers

"Termites are diploid yet eusocial — does this disprove Hamilton's rule as an explanation for eusociality?" → Answer: no — it shows haplodiploidy is not the only route; termites may have other factors (fortress defense, assured fitness) that push rB > C

"Why might a worker bee have higher inclusive fitness by raising queen's offspring (sisters, r=0.75) than raising her own offspring (r=0.5)?" → Answer: r=0.75 already exceeds 0.5 — so even if B and C were equal, Hamilton's rule tips toward helping raise sisters
Haplodiploidy is NOT required for eusociality — termites are diploid and eusocial
Workers are NOT always sterile — they can have unfertilized (male) eggs
Eusociality evolved MULTIPLE TIMES independently — it is not a single derived trait
